title: RAVEN Announces First Project: Autonomous Underwater Vehicle
excerpt: The team reveals details about its first major project: an autonomous underwater vehicle for marine research.
content:
RAVEN is thrilled to announce our first major project: developing an autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) designed specifically for marine research and environmental monitoring.

The project, codenamed "Nautilus," will challenge our team to create a fully autonomous system capable of underwater navigation, data collection, and obstacle avoidance. This ambitious undertaking combines hardware engineering, software development, and environmental science into one comprehensive project.

Key features of our AUV will include:

- Depth-rated waterproof hull capable of operating at up to 50 meters
- Multiple sensor arrays for water quality assessment and mapping
- Computer vision systems for underwater object detection and avoidance
- Machine learning algorithms for autonomous decision making
- Energy-efficient propulsion system for extended missions

One of our primary goals is to create a platform that can assist researchers in monitoring marine ecosystems, particularly in areas affected by climate change. The vehicle will be designed to collect data on water temperature, salinity, pH levels, and other critical environmental factors.

Project lead Sofia Chen explained the motivation: "We wanted our first project to have real-world impact beyond the engineering challenge. By focusing on environmental monitoring, we're building something that could contribute to important scientific research while also pushing the boundaries of what student teams can accomplish."

The project timeline extends over the next academic year, with the first pool tests scheduled for early fall. RAVEN is actively seeking new team members interested in contributing to this exciting project, particularly those with experience in embedded systems, hydrodynamics, or environmental sensing.

Interested students are invited to attend our next meeting on May 10th to learn more about how they can get involved.
